title: Writing - Part 1: Peer Pressure
description: Your class has been discussing the effects of peer pressure on young people and how schools and families can help teenagers deal with it.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the effects in your notes. You should explain which effect you consider most serious, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Young people may feel pressured into experimenting with alcohol, drugs or other dangerous activities to fit in.
The constant need to conform to group expectations can seriously damage a teenager's confidence.
Both positive and negative peer influence can affect how much effort students put into their studies.

structure.intro: Introduce the concept of peer pressure and state the two effects you will discuss.
structure.body1: Discuss the first effect and explain how it affects young people.
structure.body2: Discuss the second effect and compare its seriousness to the first.
structure.conclusion: State which effect you consider most serious and suggest how it can be addressed.

content.modelAnswer:
Peer pressure is a powerful social force that affects almost all young people at some point during their teenage years. While it can occasionally be positive, its harmful effects are widely recognised. Two of the most concerning consequences are the encouragement of risky behaviour and the damage it can cause to self-esteem.

The desire to be accepted can lead young people to engage in dangerous activities they would otherwise avoid. Experimenting with alcohol, drugs or other risky behaviours often begins as a response to social pressure rather than genuine curiosity. Once established, such habits can be extremely difficult to break and may have serious long-term health consequences.

Peer pressure also takes a significant toll on self-esteem. When young people feel that they must constantly adapt their behaviour or appearance to meet others' expectations, they lose touch with their own identity and values. This can lead to anxiety, depression and a lasting lack of confidence.

In my opinion, the impact on self-esteem is the more serious effect. While risky behaviour has obvious physical dangers, damage to a young person's sense of identity can affect every aspect of their life for years to come. Building resilience, encouraging open conversations and providing positive role models are key to helping teenagers withstand peer pressure.
